text.title: Tool-Call Schema Design Checklist Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Tool-Call Schema Design Checklist application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Checklist verifies: naming (verb-object), description (acts as prompt), bundling decision (single vs split), idempotency (write tools), structured errors, enum constraints, terse output. All seven checked before ship.
rule.rationale: Skipping items = bugs in production; checklist is the only reliable preventer.

rule.statement: Name follows verb-object: `create_invoice`, `find_user_by_email`. Avoid namespacing prefixes like `tool_invoice_create` (token cost + readability).
rule.rationale: verb-object is the empirically optimal naming convention for LLM tool selection.

rule.statement: Description is 1-3 sentences + ≥1 example call. Description directly affects tool-selection accuracy; treat as prompt.
rule.rationale: Tool description is the only signal the model has for selection; thin descriptions cause mis-selection.

rule.statement: Write-tools are idempotent (same call → same effect) OR require explicit confirmation. Non-idempotent writes cause duplicates on retry.
rule.rationale: Agents retry; non-idempotent writes create double-charges, double-emails, double-tickets.

rule.statement: Errors return structured JSON ({code, message, retriable}). Plain-prose errors confuse the model + create silent failure loops.
rule.rationale: Structured errors enable model to make routing decisions; prose errors get re-tried mindlessly.
